text.title: DORA Metrics and Adoption Roadmap
text.summary: DORA elite performance targets, the CD vs CI vs Continuous Deployment comparison matrix, and a five-phase adoption roadmap using complexity tiers instead of time estimates.

section.title: CI vs CD vs Continuous Deployment Matrix

rule.statement: When explaining the distinction between CI, CD (Delivery), and CD (Deployment), quote the matrix verbatim — agents and teams confuse them constantly.
rule.rationale: Teams that conflate Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment enable auto-deploy on services without the safety nets (canary analysis, SLO gating) that auto-deploy requires.

section.p: Build automation: CI yes, CD yes, CDeployment yes. Automated tests: CI yes, CD yes, CDeployment yes. Deploy to staging: CI no, CD yes, CDeployment yes. Production-ready: CI no, CD yes, CDeployment yes. Auto-deploy to prod: CI no, CD no (manual trigger), CDeployment yes (automatic).

rule.statement: Pair DORA elite targets with measurement instructions; quoting numbers without instrumentation creates false expectations.
rule.rationale: Teams that adopt DORA targets without wiring deploy events to a metrics store cannot tell whether they are measuring correctly, and typically over-report their performance.

rule.statement: Measure deploy frequency from successful production deployment events in the CD pipeline, not from git push events.
rule.rationale: A push that fails in CI or staging is not a production deployment; counting pushes inflates the frequency metric.

list.item: Deployment frequency (elite): on-demand, multiple per day.
Lead time for changes (elite): under 1 hour from commit to production.
Change failure rate (elite): 0-15% of deployments cause incidents.
Time to restore (elite): under 1 hour from incident detection to recovery.

rule.statement: Use complexity tiers (Low/Medium/High) and token estimates for phases, never time estimates.
rule.rationale: Project policy prohibits time estimates in SDD documents; complexity tiers communicate effort without false precision.

list.item: Phase 1 (Foundation): automated builds on every commit, unit tests in pipeline (>70% coverage), lint/type-check gates, artifacts stored. Complexity: Low.
Phase 2 (Automated Testing): integration tests automated, E2E on staging, test parallelization, pipeline p95 under 15 minutes. Complexity: Medium.
Phase 3 (Staging Deployment): auto-deploy to staging, staging mirrors production, smoke tests automated, feature flags implemented. Complexity: Medium.
Phase 4 (Production Deployment): one-click production deploy, rolling/canary deployment strategy, automated rollback, monitoring and alerting. Complexity: High.
Phase 5 (Continuous Deployment, optional): auto-deploy to production gated by SLO burn-rate, canary analysis automated, progressive delivery. Complexity: High.

rule.statement: Do not advance to Phase 5 (Continuous Deployment) without canary analysis and SLO-based rollout gates in place; enabling auto-prod-deploy without these safety nets is high-risk.
rule.rationale: Continuous Deployment requires a stronger safety net than Continuous Delivery: automated canary analysis, real-time monitoring tied to deploy events, and SLO burn-rate-based rollback.

text.reference: Per-service CD readiness scorecard covering CI, tests, IaC, release, and DORA metrics.
